bins.name: Integrated Reform Framework
bins.desc: The filer argues that connection queue backlogs and structural weaknesses in existing regimes require a coherent, multi-part policy response rather than isolated fixes. Reforms to queue management, cost allocation, flexibility, and socio-environmental screening should be designed together.
quotes: Long connection queues, disputes over reinforcement cost allocation, and the growing political sensitivity of new large-load projects have exposed structural weaknesses in existing connection regimes.

Speculative requests for connections are either not backed by a finalised project but serve as a way to hold a place in queue as the project is developed, or an existing project that applies for connections in multiple locations with the intent of building in only the one that gives them the lowest cost.

our key recommendation is that reforms should be designed as a coherent package: queue management, cost allocation, flexibility incentives and socio-environmental screening work best cohesively, rather than introduced separately.
the rapid growth of data centre demand is not inherently incompatible with grid reliability, affordability or net-zero objectives, but it does require a deliberate and integrated policy response.

bins.name: Cost Allocation to Large Loads
bins.desc: The filer documents that shallow charges shift billions in upgrade costs to existing ratepayers, raising bills and stoking opposition. It supports deep charging and bespoke contract structures that place costs on the connecting data centre while providing cost certainty for developers.
quotes: socialising connection charges ("shallow charges") means that the connecting party (i.e. a data centre) does not pay the full cost of the network upgrades, so these are instead borne by other grid users (e.g. residential users).

customers from Illinois, Maryland, New Jersey, Ohio, Pennsylvania, Virginia and West Virginia, collectively paid over US$ 4.3 billion in 2024 for grid investments that were only necessary to accommodate new data centres.

While shallow connection charges can help attract new data centre projects, they can have a negative impact in the local community's cost of living, which could then fuel counter-productive opposition.
Going in the opposite direction, Norway established in their latest policy update that a deep connection charge would be used for projects with capacity above 1 MW.

Data centres could negotiate bespoke contracts with network owners and/or operators, under regulatory approval. These contracts could be used to get the data centres to contribute to the local network to the best of its ability and could result in lower costs for the energy company and lower risks for other grid users.

bins.name: Demand Flexibility Obligations
bins.desc: The filer recognises data centres as potential flexibility assets through workload shifting and demand response, and supports contractual flexibility requirements. However, it notes that industry opposed mandatory flexible connections proposed by PJM on legal and reliability grounds, suggesting flexibility obligations should be balanced with operational concerns.
quotes: data centres can be a source of flexibility for the energy grid in the UK. Knittel et al make a similar argument for the USA.
both Data Centres and Supplier companies in the USA have opposed a proposed imposition of flexible connections by the PJM citing legality, fairness and grid reliability.

Data centres could negotiate bespoke contracts with network owners and/or operators, under regulatory approval. These contracts could be used to get the data centres to contribute to the local network to the best of its ability and could result in lower costs for the energy company and lower risks for other grid users.

bins.name: Co-location and Proximity Rules
bins.desc: The filer describes how PJM identified multiple co-location options with distinct grid impacts, and how Irish rules distinguish proximity requirements for capacity obligations versus renewable energy targets. It presents these as reference points for regulators rather than advocating a particular model.
quotes: However, the American network operator, PJM, reveals several complications in its May 2025 publication, showing 8 options for large load co-location, identifying unique grid impacts of each.

CRU does not allow a data centre to meet its capacity obligation using remote generation elsewhere in Ireland. However, it does allow remote renewable generation to count toward the 80% renewable requirement.

bins.name: Proximate Generation Requirements
bins.desc: The filer explains that Ireland requires dispatchable generation to be on-site or proximate for large data centres, while permitting remote renewable generation for the annual energy target. This distinction illustrates how proximity rules can be calibrated to different reliability and decarbonisation objectives.
quotes: CRU does not allow a data centre to meet its capacity obligation using remote generation elsewhere in Ireland. However, it does allow remote renewable generation to count toward the 80% renewable requirement.

bins.name: Upfront Fees to Deter Speculation
bins.desc: The filer endorses requiring data centres to pay upfront fees before securing a queue position, citing AEP Ohio's tariff as evidence that such requirements reduce speculative applications and protect ratepayers from stranded upgrade costs.
quotes: Speculative requests for connections are either not backed by a finalised project but serve as a way to hold a place in queue as the project is developed, or an existing project that applies for connections in multiple locations with the intent of building in only the one that gives them the lowest cost.
Requiring data centres to bear upfront costs could help minimise speculative data centre connection requests.

bins.name: Size-Based Policy Thresholds
bins.desc: The filer cites examples from Norway and Idaho where capacity thresholds trigger differentiated charges or tariffs for large loads, and supports similar size-based rules to distinguish riskier large load connections from smaller ones.
quotes: socialising connection charges ("shallow charges") means that the connecting party (i.e. a data centre) does not pay the full cost of the network upgrades, so these are instead borne by other grid users (e.g. residential users).
Going in the opposite direction, Norway established in their latest policy update that a deep connection charge would be used for projects with capacity above 1 MW.

bins.name: Bespoke Study and Contract Process
bins.desc: The filer supports allowing data centres to negotiate bespoke contracts with network companies under regulatory oversight, so that connection studies and cost-sharing arrangements reflect project-specific conditions rather than uniform rules.
quotes: Data centres could negotiate bespoke contracts with network owners and/or operators, under regulatory approval. These contracts could be used to get the data centres to contribute to the local network to the best of its ability and could result in lower costs for the energy company and lower risks for other grid users.

bins.name: PJM Region Cost and Flexibility Issues
bins.desc: The filer references PJM specifically to illustrate the scale of ratepayer-funded data centre upgrades (over $4.3 billion in 2024 in PJM-footprint states) and industry opposition to mandatory flexible connections, as well as PJM's analysis of co-location options.
quotes: both Data Centres and Supplier companies in the USA have opposed a proposed imposition of flexible connections by the PJM citing legality, fairness and grid reliability.

customers from Illinois, Maryland, New Jersey, Ohio, Pennsylvania, Virginia and West Virginia, collectively paid over US$ 4.3 billion in 2024 for grid investments that were only necessary to accommodate new data centres.
However, the American network operator, PJM, reveals several complications in its May 2025 publication, showing 8 options for large load co-location, identifying unique grid impacts of each.

bins.name: Connection Queue Management
bins.desc: The filer identifies speculative applications and first-come-first-served allocation as core drivers of long connection queues. It supports upfront fees, milestone requirements, and merit-based or auction-based prioritisation to reduce queue bloat and improve efficiency.
quotes: Long connection queues, disputes over reinforcement cost allocation, and the growing political sensitivity of new large-load projects have exposed structural weaknesses in existing connection regimes.

Speculative requests for connections are either not backed by a finalised project but serve as a way to hold a place in queue as the project is developed, or an existing project that applies for connections in multiple locations with the intent of building in only the one that gives them the lowest cost.
Requiring data centres to bear upfront costs could help minimise speculative data centre connection requests.

bins.name: Shallow vs. Deep Charging Regimes
bins.desc: The filer contrasts shallow charges (which socialise upgrade costs) with deep charges (which assign costs to the data centre). It presents evidence that shallow charging has transferred billions in costs to ratepayers and argues that deeper cost assignment better reflects the causer-pays principle.
quotes: socialising connection charges ("shallow charges") means that the connecting party (i.e. a data centre) does not pay the full cost of the network upgrades, so these are instead borne by other grid users (e.g. residential users).

customers from Illinois, Maryland, New Jersey, Ohio, Pennsylvania, Virginia and West Virginia, collectively paid over US$ 4.3 billion in 2024 for grid investments that were only necessary to accommodate new data centres.

While shallow connection charges can help attract new data centre projects, they can have a negative impact in the local community's cost of living, which could then fuel counter-productive opposition.
Going in the opposite direction, Norway established in their latest policy update that a deep connection charge would be used for projects with capacity above 1 MW.

bins.name: Data Centre Flexibility Potential
bins.desc: The filer sees data centres as valuable flexibility resources and lists multiple mechanisms (workload shifting, UPS ancillary services, spatial migration) by which they can support grid balancing. It notes regulatory and contractual barriers that currently limit uptake and flags industry resistance to mandatory requirements.
quotes: data centres can be a source of flexibility for the energy grid in the UK. Knittel et al make a similar argument for the USA.
both Data Centres and Supplier companies in the USA have opposed a proposed imposition of flexible connections by the PJM citing legality, fairness and grid reliability.

bins.name: Bespoke Large Load Contracts
bins.desc: The filer supports bespoke bilateral contracts under regulatory oversight as a way to tailor flexibility obligations and cost arrangements to specific project conditions, citing the Indiana Michigan Power and Google contract as an example of the benefits this model can deliver.
quotes: Data centres could negotiate bespoke contracts with network owners and/or operators, under regulatory approval. These contracts could be used to get the data centres to contribute to the local network to the best of its ability and could result in lower costs for the energy company and lower risks for other grid users.

bins.name: Co-location Grid Impact Complexity
bins.desc: The filer notes that PJM's analysis of eight co-location options reveals that each carries distinct grid impacts, cautioning that co-location should not be treated as a straightforward solution without careful assessment of the specific arrangement.
quotes: However, the American network operator, PJM, reveals several complications in its May 2025 publication, showing 8 options for large load co-location, identifying unique grid impacts of each.

bins.name: Bring-Your-Own-Generation Rules
bins.desc: The filer reviews the Irish BYOG framework as a model for regulation-driven on-site or proximate generation obligations and endorses BYOG as a tool that can ease capacity constraints and accelerate connections, while noting limitations in the current Irish framework's temporal and locational matching rules.
quotes: CRU does not allow a data centre to meet its capacity obligation using remote generation elsewhere in Ireland. However, it does allow remote renewable generation to count toward the 80% renewable requirement.

bins.name: Coherent Multi-Barrier Policy Packages
bins.desc: The filer's central recommendation is that barriers to data centre integration share common structural roots, so policy responses must be designed as integrated packages. It argues that data centre demand growth is compatible with reliability and net-zero if regulators treat the five barriers as interconnected.
quotes: our key recommendation is that reforms should be designed as a coherent package: queue management, cost allocation, flexibility incentives and socio-environmental screening work best cohesively, rather than introduced separately.
the rapid growth of data centre demand is not inherently incompatible with grid reliability, affordability or net-zero objectives, but it does require a deliberate and integrated policy response.
